title: Chapter 22: The Alliance
content: The door is open.

Not broken. Not kicked in. Just… open. Ajar by three inches, maybe four, the corridor's gray morning light cutting a blade across the floor of my room. I stare at it from the bed, sheets tangled around my legs, and wait for the fear to come.
It doesn't.

I've been afraid so many times in this house that the emotion has worn thin, like a record played too often, the grooves smoothed flat. Last night changed something. Flora's words — *Miles filmed her walking into the lake* — scraped me down to a layer I didn't know existed, and whatever grows back won't be the same shape.
I swing my legs over the edge of the mattress. The floor is cold through my socks. I don't bother changing. I don't bother with much anymore.

The corridor is empty. No guard outside my door. Hannah's post has been vacant since sometime in the night — or maybe she left on instruction, or maybe she left on principle. Everyone in this house serves someone. The question is whether the allegiances have shifted, or whether this is another arrangement entirely.

I make my way downstairs. The manor is the kind of quiet that has texture — a held breath, a pause between sentences. I can hear my own footsteps on the runner, the creak of the fourth step that I've learned to avoid but don't bother avoiding now. Let them hear me. Let them all hear me.
The kitchen light is on.

Mrs. Grose is at the table. Not standing at the counter, not moving between stove and sink. She is seated, both hands wrapped around a teapot that must have been steeping for a while because the air smells of bergamot and something deeper, tannic and warm. Two cups sit on the worn wooden surface. Two saucers. A small jug of milk.

She looks up when I enter. Her face is the same face it's always been — broad, weathered, careful. But there's something missing from it this morning. The watchfulness. The constant accounting of what can and cannot be said.
"Sit down," she says.
I sit.

She pours. The tea is real — actual loose-leaf Earl Grey, not the dust-bag stuff she's served me for five weeks. The cup warms my palms. The saucer rattles slightly when I set it down because my hands aren't as steady as I want them to be.
"Flora told me what she told you," Mrs. Grose says.

No preamble. No dance. I look at her over the rim of the cup and see something I've never seen in her before: exhaustion, yes, but underneath it, a kind of readiness. Like a woman standing at the edge of a high board who has finally decided to jump.

"I know," she continues, "because Flora tells me everything. She always has. Miles thinks she's his creature, his mirror, his partner. But Flora has been keeping her own accounts since she was six years old." She takes a slow drink of her tea. "She told me about Miss Jessel too. Months before anyone else knew. She told me about the lake. About what Miles made her watch."
The bergamot is sharp on my tongue. The cup is warm enough to almost sting. I wait.
"You're wondering why I didn't do anything," Mrs. Grose says. It's not a question. "You're wondering how I could know and keep knowing and still pour the tea and make the beds and—"
"I'm wondering if you'll help me now," I say.

She sets her cup down. The sound is small in the large kitchen, a gentle clink against the glaze of the saucer. The kitchen windows face east, and the light coming through them is the color of dirty wool, a morning that can't decide if it wants to be day.

"I've been waiting thirty years for someone to end this," she says. Her voice doesn't waver. It has the quality of something that has been held too long in a closed space — stale but strong. "I came to this house when Henry Wingrave's father was still alive. I watched the first Game. I watched the second. I watched them escalate, and I watched the children get younger, and I watched the money move through accounts in places that don't ask questions, and I said nothing."
She looks at me directly. Her eyes are gray-green, rheumy with age but sharp.

"I was complicit. I know that. I kept the house running because the alternative was letting the twins self-destruct without supervision. Without me, there would have been no meals, no clean linens, no one to notice when one of them didn't come home. Without me, Miles would have burned this place down years ago. And I told myself that was enough. That keeping them alive was the same as keeping them from harm."
"It wasn't," I say.
"No," she agrees. "It wasn't."

The tea is cooling. I take another sip and taste the tannins now, the slight bitterness of over-steeping. Mrs. Grose has been sitting here longer than this morning. She's been sitting here for decades, pouring tea for monsters and calling it hospitality.
"But now there's you," she says. "You're the first one who came prepared."
I almost laugh. Prepared. I came to this house with a stolen file, a dead man's name, and a head full of rage. That's not preparation. That's a suicide note written in advance.

But she's right in the way that matters. I'm not like the others — the freelancers, the gawkers, the ones Miles consumed and spat out. I came with a purpose I haven't named yet, not even to myself. And I'm still here.

"Here's what I know," I say, and I spread my fingers on the table, pressing the pads into the grain of the wood. Worn smooth by decades of elbows and plates and hands like mine, planning things in the early morning. "I don't need to destroy the twins. That's what Miles expects — someone coming for him personally, someone he can outmaneuver on his own territory. He's been playing defense against personal attacks his entire life. He's good at it."
"So what do you do?" Mrs. Grose asks.
"Destroy the Game."
I say it quietly. It sounds almost religious in this kitchen, this confirmation. A vow.

"That means four things," I continue. "One: get Peter Quint's files to the authorities. The real files, not the redacted versions Henry Wingrave fed the insurance investigators. There's enough in those files to reopen every closed inquiry into Bly Manor's finances."
"Peter kept copies," Mrs. Grose says. Not a question, but an invitation to confirm.

"Digital. Scattered across three cloud services under accounts he created before he died. I have the access keys. He left them for someone to find." The words taste like ash. He left them for me. His daughter. The thing he made before he unmade himself, or was unmade.

"Two," I say, pushing past it: "prove Jessel's death was not suicide. The autopsy was compromised — Miles would have made sure of that. But there's the footage. And there are the people who were here that night. Someone talked to Miss Jessel before she walked into that water. Someone gave her a reason."
"Rebecca," Mrs. Grose murmurs. The first name. It lands on the table between us like something fragile. "Her name was Rebecca Jessel."

"Rebecca," I repeat. I owe her that much — the specificity of her name. "Three: expose Henry Wingrave's knowledge and cover-up. He knew what happened to Rebecca. He knew what the Game was. He signed off on the narrative — suicide, tragic accident, nothing to see — because the alternative was losing the income stream and the family reputation in the same stroke."

"Henry's a coward," Mrs. Grose says. It's the most contempt I've heard in her voice. "Cowards are easier to crack than villains. Give him a clear exit and he'll take it, trampling everyone behind him."
"That's what I'm counting on."
"And four?"

"Timing." I tap the table once, firmly. "All of this has to happen before Miles can counter. He's fast — faster than anyone gives him credit for. He'll have contingencies, fallback positions, escape routes coded into the architecture of his life. We need to move before he finishes adapting to Flora's defection. We need to move while he still thinks the board is his."
Mrs. Grose is quiet for a moment. She lifts her cup, drinks, sets it down. The kitchen clock ticks somewhere behind me, measuring seconds we may not have.
"The files I can get," she says. "Peter hid physical copies too, in the east wing. I know which rooms. I've known for years."
"Why didn't you—"

"Because I was waiting for someone who could use them." She meets my eyes. "The files aren't enough. You know that. People have tried before. Documents in a vacuum are just paper. You need the story. You need people to care."
A floorboard creaks in the doorway.

We both turn. Flora stands there in a shapeless gray sweatshirt two sizes too large, her hair uncombed, her feet bare on the tile. She looks younger than fourteen. She looks older. The paradox of children who have seen too much — they shrink and expand simultaneously, like a photograph exposed and then overexposed, the image burning through the paper.
"I'll help," she says.

She says it the way someone says *I'm still breathing* — a fact, not a boast. She moves into the kitchen and stands at the end of the table, her hands tucked into the sleeves of the sweatshirt. Her nails, I notice, are bitten to the quick. The quick and past it, tiny half-moons of red at the corners.
"But I need something in return," she adds.
I wait. Mrs. Grose waits. The clock ticks.
"What?" I ask.
Flora pulls her hands out of her sleeves. They're pale, the knuckles slightly rough. She places them flat on the table, one beside the other, as if taking an oath.
"After this is over," she says, "I want to go to a real school. I want to have friends my own age. I want to stop being interesting."

The words hang in the kitchen air. Not dramatic. Not theatrical. Just a girl, half-whispered, asking for the thing that every other fourteen-year-old in the world is trying to escape from. Ordinary. She wants ordinary. She wants a life so unremarkable that no one would ever think to film it.

I think of my own childhood — the dull suburbs, the strip malls, the suffocating normalcy I spent my teenage years clawing to get away from. I would have traded it for the excitement of Bly Manor. I would have been a fool.
"Done," I say.

Flora nods once. Her hands stay flat on the table. They're cold — I can see the faint blue of the veins at her wrists, the way her fingertips are bloodless. The manor is always cold. It's a house built to preserve things, not to warm them.

She pulls out a chair and sits. The scrape of wood on tile sounds like a small declaration. Mrs. Grose rises without being asked and retrieves a third cup from the cabinet. Pours. Sets it in front of Flora. The girl wraps her hands around it and doesn't drink.

"The livestream gave us attention," Flora says. She's switched registers — from the girl asking for normalcy to the strategist who grew up inside the machine. "Forty-seven thousand concurrent viewers at peak. The clip of Miles talking about Rebecca has been viewed six million times across platforms. People are interested."
"They're interested in ghosts," I say.
"They're interested in the story," Flora corrects. "The ghosts are the wrapper."

Mrs. Grose shifts in her chair. She's been quiet during this exchange, watching the two of us with an expression I can't fully parse. Caution, maybe. Or recognition — she's seeing something she's seen before, two versions of the same instinct sizing each other up.

"There's a problem," Mrs. Grose says. "The guests. The ones the children have been seeing for years — the woman by the lake, the figure on the tower, the presence in the schoolroom. They're not ghosts."
"We know that," I say.

"They're performances," she continues, as if I haven't spoken. "Stagecraft. The twins created them, amplified them, let the narrative build. Some of it was projection — lights, sounds, the right suggestion at the right moment. Some of it was more elaborate. Miles has contacts in the special effects industry. He hired people, seasonally, to create incidents that the audience could interpret as they wished."
"Flora told me about the woman by the lake."

"Miss Jessel. Yes." Mrs. Grose's voice catches, just barely, on the name. "After Rebecca died, the ghost story became useful. It provided an explanation that wasn't murder. The tragedy of a woman who walked into the water — why? Grief? Madness? A haunted house that drives its residents to despair? Pick your narrative. They all serve the same purpose: they turn a crime into a mystery, and a mystery into content."

"But they serve another purpose too," I say slowly. The idea is forming as I speak it, rising from somewhere I don't want to examine too closely. "The ghosts maintain the story. Without the ghost narrative, the internet loses interest."
Mrs. Grose nods. "Now you see it."
I do see it. And what I see makes my stomach tighten.

A dead woman in a lake is a tragedy. A dead woman in a lake at a haunted manor, where spectral figures appear on camera and shadowy shapes move in the background of livestreams — that's a phenomenon. That's something people share and reinterpret and deep-dive and dissect. That's something the algorithms promote and the comment sections argue about and the podcast episodes multiply around.
Without the ghosts, Rebecca Jessel is just another victim. Another name. Another statistic in a file that gets closed and shelved.
With the ghosts, she's the center of a story the whole world is watching.

"I need the ghosts," I say. The words feel wrong in my mouth, like speaking a language I learned from a book without knowing the pronunciation. "The supernatural narrative keeps the public engaged. I have to keep the ghost story alive — even knowing it's manufactured — to keep the eyes on Bly. The moment people stop believing there's something supernatural here, they stop caring. And the moment they stop caring, Miles wins. He just has to wait for the attention to die."
Flora is watching me. Her cup is still full, the tea going cold between her palms.
"So we keep lying," she says.

It's not an accusation. It's an assessment. The newest ex-member of the Game, evaluating the new strategy with the cold pragmatism of someone who learned to think this way before she learned to think any other way.

"We keep the story running," I say. "The true story, underneath. The supernatural framing is just the vehicle. We use it to deliver the evidence — the files, the footage, the testimony. We give them the ghost story and then we show them the body beneath it."

The clock ticks. Mrs. Grose drinks her tea. Flora turns her cup slowly between her hands, not looking at either of us. The light through the eastern windows has warmed slightly — or maybe I've just been in this kitchen long enough to notice it.

"Three of us," Mrs. Grose says. She's tallying. She's been tallying for thirty years. It's what she does. "Against Miles. Against the Wingrave estate. Against a machine that has been refining itself since before Flora was born."
"Three of us," I confirm.
"Miles will know. He may already know. He monitors this house more closely than any of us realize."
"Then we don't give him time to adapt."
Mrs. Grose sets her cup down one final time. The saucer accepts it with a soft click, ceramic on ceramic, a sound like a lock turning over.
"I'm in," she says.

Flora doesn't speak. She lifts her cup, finally, and drinks. The tea must be cold by now, but she swallows it without flinching. When she sets the cup down, there is a faint trace of it on her lower lip, and she wipes it away with the back of her hand like a child who hasn't been taught manners, or like someone who was taught them too young and has chosen to forget.
"I'm in," she echoes.
Three women against a boy and a system. It should feel triumphant. It should feel like the turning point, the moment the tide shifts, the chapter where everything starts to change.
It doesn't.
It feels like standing on a cliff's edge and choosing to step forward. Not because you can fly, but because the alternative is standing still forever.

Mrs. Grose rises and begins collecting the cups. I watch her move through the kitchen — efficiently, automatically, the body memory of ten thousand mornings. She knows where everything goes. She knows how this house works. She's been its circulatory system for three decades, pumping meals and laundry and silence through rooms that would otherwise fill with rot.
Can I trust her? She's been complicit. She's been the one who looked away. But complicity and loyalty aren't the same thing, and the distance between them is where people change.
Flora stays seated. She traces the grain of the table with one finger — the worn wood, the scratches and dents of years, the map of a house that has seen everything and recorded nothing.
"Maeve," she says quietly.
I look at her.
"He's going to try to turn you. Miles. He'll tell you the Game is what you're made for. He'll say it's in your blood." She pauses. Her finger stops on a dark groove in the wood. "He might be right."
The words land. I let them.

There is a part of me — the part that is Peter Quint's daughter, the part that walked into this house with a stolen file and a plan that has more moving parts than I've admitted to anyone — that understands the Game. Not just how to resist it. How to play it. How to shape it. The strategy, the psychology, the architecture of manipulation that makes people move where you want them to move without knowing they've been guided.
I've been telling myself it's necessity. That the ends justify the mechanisms. That I'm not like Miles because my motives are clean.
But motives don't sanitize methods. And somewhere in the last thirty-five days, I've stopped flinching at the calculations. I've stopped asking whether I'm becoming something I should fear.
I stand. The kitchen chair scrapes back. Mrs. Grose is at the sink, her back to us, water running. Flora's finger has resumed its slow journey across the wood.

"We start tomorrow," I say. "Tonight we gather what we need. Mrs. Grose, the files in the east wing — I need to know exactly where. Flora, I need every camera angle in this house, every blind spot, every feed that Miles monitors and every one he doesn't."
Mrs. Grose turns off the tap. Flora nods without looking up.
I walk out of the kitchen and back into the corridor, where the manor's silence settles around me like dust. The fourth step creaks under my weight. I let it.
Three women against a machine. The alliance is formed.

But alliances are built on the fiction that the people inside them are fixed — that Mrs. Grose will stay rebellious, that Flora will stay brave, that I will stay principled. And people aren't fixed. They're currents. They shift with the pressure of what's around them.

I need the ghosts to keep the world watching. I need to sustain the supernatural narrative — the lie — to deliver the truth. I need to manipulate the audience the same way Miles manipulates them, just in a different direction.
The question isn't whether I can navigate the contradiction. The question is whether I'll know when I've stopped navigating and started believing.
The corridor stretches ahead. The doors on either side are closed. Behind any one of them, Miles could be watching, listening, planning.
I walk toward the stairs. I don't look back.
The ghost story continues.
